IDG functional pathways are highlighted for Tclin and Tchem categories. Associated datasets are provided in Supplementary Tables 1–4 and 6 and 7. Source data PTM-dependent targets include druggable protein classes such as enzymes and ion channels but also historically more challenging targets, including epigenetic regulators and transcription factors (Fig. 2d)28. Gene Ontology analysis revealed that proteins displaying PTM-dependent ligandability are enriched in cellular processes distinct from those that do not. Although non-targets (probe-enriched proteins that do not display PTM dependency) were largely associated with translation and metabolism, phosphorylation-dependent targets were enriched in pathways linked to protein localization, lysosome organization, ion homeostasis and mRNA transport, whereas saccharide metabolic processes and the ERAD pathway were prominently enriched among N-glycosylation-dependent targets (Extended Data Fig. 2f,g,i,j). Critically, PTM-dependent targets were enriched (74% in phosphorylation and 62% in N-linked glycosylation) with proteins annotated to possess the corresponding PTMs (Extended Data Fig. 2h,k), suggesting that PTM status directly impacts ligandability. Finally, PTM-dependent targets include therapeutically relevant proteins (29, classified as Tclin), such as KRAS and HMGCR, and highly liganded proteins (68, classified as Tchem); however, the vast majority (approximately 78%) were found to lack well-annotated ligands (Fig. 2e). We next sought to orthogonally validate the state-dependent interactions for 10 representative targets through probe pull-down and immunoblotting. Among the STR-dependent targets, we confirmed broad reduced ligandability of the tyrosine kinase JAK1 across multiple chemotypes (P2, (R)-P6 and (S)-P6) (Fig. 3a,b). By contrast, the E1 subunit of the V-ATPase proton pump, ATP6V1E1, despite being enriched by all probes, displayed increased ligandability exclusively with (R/S)-P6, suggesting that P6 may possess a distinct binding mode that is affected by STR (Fig. 3c,d). Additional examples include the major protein kinase C substrate MARCKS (Extended Data Fig. 3a) and the transmembrane glycoprotein CD44 (Extended Data Fig. 3b), which displayed increased and decreased ligandability with P2, respectively, as well as the E3 ubiquitin-protein ligase WWP2, which exhibited decreased binding ability with (R/S)-P6 after STR treatment (Extended Data Fig. 3c). Finally, dose-dependent probe labeling for a subset of targets (ATP6V1E1, EphA2, LAMTOR2, NPC2 and EDEM3) confirmed retention of state-selective binding over a broad range of concentrations (Extended Data Fig. 3d−m). Proteomic profiles and corresponding chemoprecipitation (ChP) validation for selected targets exhibiting PTM-sensitive probe (100 µM) binding: JAK1 (a,b), ATP6V1E1 (c,d), EDEM3 (e,f) and CDH2 (g,h). Panels a, c, eand g show quantification of chemoproteomic profiling; panels b,d,f and h present validation by ChP (left) and correlated quantification across biological replicates (right). Proteomic data and immunoblot quantification derived from two independent biological replicates are presented as the mean with individual data points overlaid. Associated datasets are provided in Supplementary Tables 1–8. Source data Among phosphorylation-dependent targets, 72 (31%) harbored phosphosites quantified in our phosphoproteomic experiments, enabling more detailed analyses. Of these, 34 displayed concordant changes in phosphorylation and ligandability (Extended Data Fig. 4a,b). For example, we observed significantly reduced phosphorylation at S686, S697 and S706 on the cell surface receptor CD44 and a concomitant decrease in binding with P2, whereas we observed increased phosphorylation on S144 of chromatin-associated protein NUCKS1 and increased binding with P2. We also observed 22 instances of ligandability changes that occur directionally opposite to observed phosphorylation changes. For example, the calmodulin-binding protein MARCKS displayed increased binding with probe P2 and decreased phosphorylation at S81 and T150. Finally, we also identified 16 targets without significant phosphorylation changes, including CLN3, a lysosomal protein with strong genetic links to Batten disease29, where all reported phosphorylation sites were quantified in our phosphoproteomic experiments but were unaltered. Such instances indicate that differences in probe labeling likely occur indirectly from changes in phosphorylation status. Among the glycosylation-dependent targets, we confirmed broadly increased ligandability across all probes for EDEM3 (Fig. 3e,f), an endoplasmic reticulum-localized glycoprotein with seven annotated N-linked glycosites30. We also validated CDH2, a cell membrane-localized adhesion glycoprotein (Fig. 3g,h), and DDOST, a subunit of the oligosaccharyltransferase (OST) complex, which, in contrast to EDEM3, displayed selective increased binding to P2 (Extended Data Fig. 5a) upon Tuni treatment. We noted a subset of targets (for example, HSPA5, HYOU1 and PDIA4) that were preferentially enriched by all six probes. Further inspection revealed that these events are largely related to changes in protein abundance and consist of members of the unfolded protein response (UPR), in line with previous reports demonstrating that Tuni activates this pathway (Extended Data Fig. 5b)31. We also identified 21 functionally diverse proteins that exhibit differential ligandability under both phosphorylation and glycosylation perturbations, often in divergent directions (Extended Data Fig. 5c,d), underscoring the complexity of PTM influence over small-molecule−protein interactions. We and others have shown that integration of stereo-chemical features into probe design facilitates the confident assignment of authentic ligandable pockets on proteins20,32,33. As preluded in our gel-based profiles (Fig. 1d,f and Supplementary Fig. 1), we identified 62 ‘emergent' stereo-selective liganding events across both PTM perturbations (Fig. 2a,b and Extended Data Fig. 6a,b), wherein stereo-selective binding was found to occur in only one condition or state (34 in phosphorylation, 28 in glycosylation). Instances of emergent stereo-selective events in the phosphorylation dataset include CETN2, HIGD1A and EPHB4 (Extended Data Fig. 6c−e) as well as AS3MT, SLC25A4 and MPV17 (Extended Data Fig. 6f−h) in the glycosylation dataset. These data suggest that dynamic protein states can give rise to conditional stereo-selective liganding events. We recently developed a quantitative chemical proteomic workflow to determine, with high resolution, proteome-wide binding sites of photoaffinity probes in cells (Supplementary Fig. 3)21,34. Using this method under the PTM perturbations described above, we identified differentially labeled binding sites (P < 0.05) on 82 and 55 proteins, respectively, for previously identified phosphorylation-dependent and glycosylation-dependent targets (Supplementary Data 1). We calculated the minimal spatial distance between labeled sequences and UniProt-annotated PTM sites based on experimental or predicted structural information. This analysis revealed that, perhaps not surprisingly, binding sites exhibiting PTM dependency are generally proximal to corresponding UniProt-annotated PTM sites (Fig. 4a, Supplementary Fig. 4a,b and Supplementary Data 1), suggestive that phosphate or N-glycan modifications may directly hinder or facilitate interactions between small molecules and proteins. We observed that differentially liganded sites often occurred near annotated functional sites (<10 Å), including active, ion-binding, cofactor-binding and nucleotide-binding sites, implicating potential roles of PTM status on protein function for these targets (Extended Data Fig. 7a). Notably, in the phosphorylation dataset, PTM-dependent sites were additionally enriched near non-canonical functional regions such as cleavage sites, reactive sites and key protein−protein interaction sites (Supplementary Fig. 4c−l). a, Spatial distance distributions between probe-labeled peptides and Uniprot-annotated phosphorylation (left) and N-linked glycosylation (right) sites. b,c, Profiling and docking show that (R)-P6 binds HSPB1 near S82 (b, PDB: 6VD5), which is dephosphorylated upon STR treatment (c). d,e, Profiling and docking show that (R)-P6 binds CTSD near S37 (d, AF-P07339-F1), which undergoes STR-induced dephosphorylation (e). f,g, P2 labels GABARAPL2 near S87/88 (f, PDB: 7KL3), validated by chemoprecipitation (ChP) with wild-type (WT) and S87/88A mutant (g). h−k, Ligandability profiling and ChP validation of LAMTOR2 with (R)-P6 after STR treatment (h). i, Docked structure of LAMTOR2 (AF-Q9Y2Q5-F1) with (R)-P6 overlaps with the LAMTOR1 interface in the Ragulator complex (PDB: 5X6V). STR induces LAMTOR1 S26 dephosphorylation (j) and reduces LAMTOR1−LAMTOR2 complex formation (k). l−o, Ligandability profiling and ChP of EphA2 with (S)-P6 after STR treatment (l). Binding is competed by ALW-II-41-27 (m), as both share a site near the ATP pocket (n, PDB: 5EK7). STR induces EphA2 Y772 dephosphorylation (o). p−s, Ligandability profiling and ChP reveal PTM-selective and stereo-selective (R)-P5 binding to NPC2 after Tuni treatment (p). (R)-P5 binds the cholesterol pocket near glycosylated N58 (q), competed by cholesterol (r). N58 mutation disrupts (R)-P5 binding (s). Phosphorylation experiments were conducted in MDA-MB-231 cells with or without STR treatment (250 nM, 3 hours). Glycosylation experiments were conducted with or without Tuni (10 µg ml−1, 24 hours) in HEK293T cells. All structures depict probe-labeled residues in dark blue; the remainder of each peptide in light blue; PTM sites in red (annotated: sites assigned in UniProt; observed: sites detected in phosphoproteomics); functional residues in black; and probes in gold. Proteomics and immunoblots represent the mean of two (data points overlaid) or three (±s.d.) independent biological replicates. Significance was determined using a two-tailed Student's t-test. Associated datasets are provided in Supplementary Tables 11 and 12. Chol., cholesterol; h, hours; IP, immunoprecipitation. Source data We noted that a portion of identified sites overlapped with predicted small-molecule binding pockets (Extended Data Fig. 7b). Interestingly, we observed that pockets displaying PTM dependency generally overlap less frequently with predicted small-molecule binding pockets relative to pockets that do not display PTM dependency, suggesting that observed ligandability shifts occur within more dynamic sites rather than predictable, static pockets (Supplementary Fig. 4m,n). To begin to better understand molecular recognition components that might influence probe binding, we integrated our state-dependent site mapping data with molecular docking. We obtained atomic structures for a subset of interactions (57 structures from the Protein Data Bank (PDB) and 45 from AlphaFold) and then blindly docked every probe into every predicted pocket of unmodified protein structures and cross-referenced the results to the proteomics data21. We identified low-energy probe binding modes across a variety of pockets proximal to corresponding PTMs. These docked structures include diverse functional sites, such as ATP binding sites (TGFBR1), calcium binding sites (NUCB2 and CALM3) and phosphosites (COPE, HMGB1 and CD63) in phosphorylation-dependent pockets (Supplementary Fig. 5a−f) and protease cleavage sites (HBA1), substrate binding sites (HS6ST2), enzyme active sites (CTSB) and glycosites (OSTM1) in glycosylation-dependent pockets (Supplementary Fig. 5g−j). Consulting matched phosphoproteomic measurements, we identified 20 binding pockets with altered ligandability within 10 Å of phosphosites exhibiting STR-dependent changes. Of these, six exhibited ligandability changes that correlated with phosphorylation changes, whereas the other 14 showed the opposite trend (Extended Data Fig. 7c). For instance, we observed selective increased binding of (R)-P6 with the heat shock protein HSPB1 (Fig. 4b) in a pocket proximal (approximately 5 Å) to S82, which decreases in phosphorylation (Fig. 4c), suggesting that the phosphate group may directly obstruct probe binding. By contrast, for CTSD, both (R)-P6 binding (Fig. 4d) and phosphorylation at S37 (approximately 6 Å) decreased (Fig. 4e), indicating that the phosphate group may, in some cases, facilitate probe binding. We also observed increased P2 binding to a pocket near S87/S88 on GABARAPL2, phosphosites implicated in the autophagic roles of GABARAPL2 (ref. 35) (Fig. 4f). Mutation of these residues (S87/S88A) to block phosphorylation led to substantial increased P2 binding, confirming the impact of phosphorylation on the ligandability of this site (Fig. 4g). In addition to PTM-proximal pockets, we also identified several differentially liganded sites near protein−protein interfaces. For instance, we observed increased (S)-P5 binding to a pocket that is located at the interface of the GSTP1 homodimer and proximal to the glutathione binding site (Extended Data Fig. 7d). Interestingly, phosphorylation of GSTP1 shifts the dimer−monomer equilibrium and promotes the formation of the GSTP1−JNK complex36, suggesting that dephosphorylation may result in the formation of a neopocket at the dimer interface that promotes probe binding. Consistent with this, we observed increased formation of GSTP1 dimers and multimers upon STR treatment (Extended Data Fig. 7e,f). However, we were unable to detect GSTP1 phosphorylation and, therefore, cannot assert whether these effects are caused by changes in GSTP1 phosphorylation status or other indirect effects. We also observed an increase in (R)-P6 binding of the metalloprotease inhibitor TIMP3 after STR treatment (Extended Data Fig. 7g), although TIMP3 is itself not annotated to be phosphorylated. Binding site inspection and molecular docking revealed that (R)-P6 likely binds to a pocket at the binding interface of TNF-converting enzyme (TACE), whose activity is inhibited upon TIMP3 binding37. Interestingly, we observed a significant decrease in TACE phosphorylation at S791 (Extended Data Fig. 7h) upon STR treatment, and mutation of S791 (S791A) resulted in substantial reduction in the TIMP3−TACE interaction as well as a concomitant increase in (R)-P6 binding (Extended Data Fig. 7i,j). Together, these data suggest that increased (R)-P6 binding of TIMP3 emanates from decreased stabilization of the TIMP3−TACE complex (and, therefore, exposing a ligandable pocket), which is mediated by S791 phosphorylation status. In another example, we observed increased binding of (R)-P6 to LAMTOR2, a subunit of the Ragulator−Rag complex38, upon STR treatment (Fig. 4h and Supplementary Fig. 5k). Site mapping and docking suggest that the probe binds a pocket on LAMTOR2 that is occupied by LAMTOR1 in the intact Ragulator complex (Fig. 4i). Although several phosphosites have been reported in the Ragulator complex, primarily on LAMTOR1, we detected decreased phosphorylation only at S26 (Fig. 4j), which is distal to the (R)-P6-labeled site. This prompted us to test whether STR treatment may impact complex stability. Indeed, STR treatment substantially reduced LAMTOR1−LAMTOR2 interactions (Fig. 4k), suggesting that ligandability differences arise from disruption of the Ragulator complex and exposure binding pocket. From the glycosylation dataset, we observed an increased binding of P2 to calreticulin (CALR), a chaperone with specificity for monoglucosylated oligosaccharides, such as those of HLA-A, a component of the major histocompatibility complex class I (MHC I) complex39. Site mapping on CALR revealed increased binding at the glycoprotein interaction region, and structural docking showed that P2 occupies the same pocket as the N-linked glycan on N110 of HLA-A (Supplementary Fig. 5l), suggesting that substrate engagement may occlude probe binding. As previously noted, we observed that a subset of proteins exhibited ligandability changes under both the STR-treated and Tuni-treated conditions (Extended Data Fig. 5c,d). Although these events occur at distinct binding sites, we identified cases where the same pocket displayed opposing ligandability changes depending on the treatment. For example, we mapped the binding site of both P2 and (S)-P6 to a shared pocket near the intracellular death domain of TNFRSF10B (DR5), the receptor for the cytotoxic ligand TNFSF10/TRAIL (Extended Data Fig. 7k). STR treatment reduced binding to P2, whereas Tuni treatment increased binding with (S)-P6 at this site. Although TNFRSF10B lacks UniProt-annotated PTMs, it contains Asn-X-Ser/Thr (‘X' denotes any amino acid but Pro) canonical N-glycosylation sequons and reported phosphosites (T381/Y383) within the death domain40. Although we did not identify any phosphopeptides from TNFRSF10B in our phosphoproteomic analysis, STR treatment decreased its phosphorylation levels detected via immunoblot (Extended Data Fig. 7l). Consistent with this, P2 labeling was substantially reduced in cells recombinantly expressing T381A/Y383A TNFRSF10B (Extended Data Fig. 7m), phenocopying STR treatment. Conversely, PNGase F treatment increased (S)-P6 binding (Extended Data Fig. 7n), mirroring the effect of Tuni. Although it remains unclear whether these effects arise from modification changes in TNFRSF10B or its interactors (for example, TNFSF10), these observations highlight the dynamic nature of pocket ligandability and the context-dependent effects of PTM status. Among mapped binding pockets displaying PTM-dependent ligandability, several have known ligands, leading us to inquire whether changes in PTM status could affect binding of these ligands. For example, we identified a selective decrease in binding of probe (S)-P6 with the receptor tyrosine kinase EphA2 in the presence of STR (Fig. 4l). We identified two (S)-P6-labeled peptides on EphA2 located near the ATP binding pocket and binding site of ALW-II-41-27 (Supplementary Fig. 6a), an ATP-competitive EphA2 inhibitor41. We first validated this finding via immunoblotting (Fig. 4l and Supplementary Fig. 6b) and confirmed that the observed decrease in probe binding was not due to competitive binding with STR itself (Supplementary Fig. 6c). However, co-incubation of (S)-P6 with increasing concentrations of ALW-II-41-27 revealed clear blockade of probe labeling, indicating that they likely engage the same pocket (Fig. 4m), supported by binding site mapping and molecular docking (Fig. 4n). ALW-II-41-27 inhibitory activity decreases when Y772 on EphA2 is dephosphorylated42, aligning with our observation that STR treatment lowers Y772 phosphorylation and correspondingly decreases (S)-P6 binding (Fig. 4o). Among glycosylation-dependent targets, we identified NPC intracellular cholesterol transporter 2 (NPC2) (Fig. 4p), which facilitates lysosomal cholesterol egress, and mutations in NPC2 as well as NPC1 are associated with Niemann−Pick C disease43. We observed a stereo-selective decrease in (R)-P5 binding in Tuni-treated cells (Fig. 4p and Supplementary Fig. 6d), and binding site mapping and docking revealed that (R)-P5 engages the cholesterol-binding pocket (Fig. 4q). Consistent with this, co-incubation with cholesterol resulted in a dose-dependent decrease in (R)-P5 labeling (Fig. 4r and Supplementary Fig. 6e). We initially considered that reduced glycosylation may alter NPC2 lysosomal localization but observed minimal changes in localization after Tuni treatment (Supplementary Fig. 6f). NPC2 contains two reported N-linked glycosylation sites (N58 and N135)44. Structural analysis shows that N58 is proximal to the (R)-P5 site and the cholesterol-binding pocket (Fig. 4q), suggesting a direct role in ligand binding. Indeed, we observed that (R)-P5 binding was substantially reduced in the N58A mutant but not in the N135A mutant (Fig. 4s), suggesting that N-glycosylation on N58 may play a crucial role in small-molecule interactions of this pocket. Among targets with functional ligands displaying phosphorylation-dependent ligandability, we identified significant reduction of P3 binding to the small GTPase KRAS (Fig. 5a, Extended Data Fig. 8a and Supplementary Data 1). RAS family proteins, including KRAS, are molecular switches, receiving signals from growth factor receptors and relaying them to downstream effector pathways, which regulate processes such as cell growth and proliferation45. Somatic mutations on KRAS are the most common activating lesions found in cancer cells, with the most frequent occurring at codons 12 (G12), 13 (G13) and 61 (Q61), resulting in abnormal activation of the RAS pathway46. Substantial efforts have been made over the past decades to drug KRAS, particularly targeting oncogenic variants45,47. a,b, Proteomic profile (a) and chemoprecipitation (ChP) validation (b) of P3 binding to KRASG13D after STR treatment (250 nM, 3 hours, n = 2). c, Docked KRAS−P3 interaction near the SII pocket (PDB: 8B00), with P3-labeled residues shown in blue, phosphorylation sites (Y32 and Y64) in red, P3 in yellow and GDP in cyan. d, Dose-dependent P3 competition by the pan-KRAS inhibitor BI-2865. e, KRAS phosphorylation level decreased after STR (250 nM, 3 hours) treatment. HA-tagged KRAS was immunoprecipitated, and phosphorylation status was assessed by immunoblotting using a pan-phosphotyrosine antibody. f, ChP shows reduced P3 binding to Y32F and Y64F mutants of KRASG13D. g, Increased P3 engagement with KRASG13D upon SHP2 inhibition by SHP099 (500 nM, 30 minutes). h, CETSA reveals decreased KRAS stabilization by BI-2865 in the presence of STR, whereas SHP099 has no observable effect. Data are presented as mean with 95% confidence intervals for curve fits. i, RBD assay shows that STR diminishes the ability of KRAS-OFF inhibitors to disrupt the active (GTP-bound) KRAS−RAF interaction across multiple KRAS-mutant cell lines, whereas SHP099 restores the effect. Immunoprecipitation (IP) reveals that STR enhances KRAS−CYPA ternary complex formation in the presence of the KRAS-ON state inhibitor RMC-7977. j, Total pERK (T202/Y204) levels assessed by immunoblotting in cells co-treated with dasatinib (1 µM) and indicated KRAS inhibitors restore pERK across various KRAS mutations in multiple cell lines. k,l, Co-IP shows that the KRAS−SOS1 interaction is enhanced by Y32F/Y64F mutation (k) and STR treatment (l) but attenuated by SHP099 (l). m, In vitro fluorescence-based nucleotide exchange assay demonstrates that phosphorylation impedes SOS1-mediated GDP release, as mono-phosphorylated (pKRAS) and di-phosphorylated (ppKRAS) forms exhibit slower exchange kinetics compared to non-phosphorylated KRAS (KRAS:SOS 10:1). n, Quantification of the fold change in exchange rates (+SOS1/−SOS1). Proteomics and immunoblots represent the mean of two (data points overlaid) or three (±s.d.) independent biological replicates. See Supplementary Tables 2 and 13 for related datasets. CI, confidence interval; Tm, melting temperature; KRASi, KRAS inhibitor; min, minutes; WT, wild-type. Source data We first confirmed the identified P3 ligandability change against both endogenous KRASG13D in MDA-MB-231 cells48 (Fig. 5b and Extended Data Fig. 8b) and recombinantly expressed HA−KRASG13D in Lenti-X 293T cells over a broad range of P3 concentrations (Supplementary Fig. 7a and Extended Data Fig. 8c,d). We also observed similar phosphorylation-dependent engagement with additional common mutants, including G12C and Q61H (Extended Data Fig. 8e,f and Supplementary Fig. 7a,b). By contrast, we observed directionally opposite P3 binding to KRASWT (endogenous and recombinantly expressed) relative to the oncogenic mutants (Extended Data Fig. 8g) upon STR treatment. Moreover, cellular thermal shift assay (CETSA)49 experiments using the unfunctionalized parent 6-aminocoumarin confirmed that this phosphorylation-dependent interaction extends to the binding of the fragment itself (Extended Data Fig. 8h,i). We mapped the binding site of P3 to a region adjacent to the switch-II (SII) pocket (Fig. 5c). Molecular docking to GDP-bound KRAS (PDB: 8B00) yielded a binding pose that overlaps with several KRAS inhibitors50,51,52,53, including BI-2865 and AMG-510 (Supplementary Fig. 7c,d), and this assignment was supported by competition experiments in cells, where both inhibitors dose-dependently blocked probe binding (Fig. 5d and Extended Data Fig. 8j). Although these data localize P3 binding to the SII region and indicate overlap with inhibitor binding sites, we cannot unambiguously distinguish between occupation of the canonical SII pocket and the adjacent SI/SII pocket, both of which are known to accommodate ligands54. Consistent with engagement of this regulatory interface, we observed that GppNHp, a non-hydrolyzable analog of GTP, blocks P3 binding to KRASG13D, whereas GDP had no observable effect, demonstrating that P3 preferentially binds to the GDP-bound state (Extended Data Fig. 8k). Inspection of P3's binding mode revealed its proximity to Y32 and Y64 (<10 Å), raising the possibility that phosphorylation of these sites contributes to the observed differential binding. Consistent with this, STR led to dephosphorylation of KRASG13D (Fig. 5e), and phosphorylation-deficient Y32F and Y64F mutants showed ablated P3 binding to both G13D and G12C mutants, consistent with the effects observed with STR treatment of tyrosine counterparts (Fig. 5f and Extended Data Fig. 8l). Pharmacological inhibition of the KRAS phosphatase SHP2 with SHP099 (refs. 55,56) modestly increased P3 engagement in both MDA-MB-231 (KRASG13D) cells (Fig. 5g) and HCC44 (KRASG12C) cells (Extended Data Fig. 8e). Conversely, inhibition of c-Src kinase with dasatinib, which phosphorylates KRAS at Y32 and Y64 (refs. 56,57), reduced P3 binding (Supplementary Fig. 7e). Together, these data establish that phosphorylation at Y32 and Y64 modulates ligand engagement proximal to the SII pocket. We next wondered whether phosphorylation would also affect the interaction between KRAS and SWII pocket inhibitors. Using CETSA, we found that BI-2865 induced robust thermal stabilization of KRAS in MDA-MB-231 cells, which was effectively diminished when the cells were co-treated with STR or dasatinib (Fig. 5h and Supplementary Fig. 7f,g). By contrast, treatment with SHP099 restored and even enhanced the stabilizing effects (Fig. 5h and Supplementary Fig. 7f), indicating that KRAS phosphorylation status influences inhibitor engagement. Because SWII inhibitors block interactions with c-Raf51,53,58, we tested whether phosphorylation status impacts this activity. We observed a dose-dependent disruption of the RAS−RAF interaction in cells treated with BI-2865; however, this disruption was ablated upon STR treatment (Fig. 5i and Supplementary Fig. 8a), an effect sustained for at least 8 hours (Extended Data Fig. 9a). Critically, BI-2865 failed to disrupt KRAS−RAF in Y32F (KRASG13D-Y32F) and Y64F (KRASG13D-Y64F) mutants58 (Extended Data Fig. 9b), consistent with the hypothesis that KRAS phosphorylation at these sites impacts inhibitor binding. Similar trends were observed with another non-covalent inhibitor (BI-2493) as well as covalent inhibitors (AMG-510 and MRTX-849), whose activities were likewise diminished in cells treated with STR (Fig. 5i and Supplementary Fig. 8a) or dasatinib (Extended Data Fig. 9c,d). We observed similar effects with BI-2865-treated or BI-2493-treated H460 cells harboring the Q61H mutation, which exhibits impaired intrinsic and GAP-mediated GTP hydrolysis59,60,61, possibly indicating that phosphorylation-dependent modulation of inhibitor engagement is not explained solely by altered nucleotide cycling (Extended Data Fig. 9e and Supplementary Fig. 8a). Intriguingly, treatment of cells with RMC-7977, which targets GTP-bound KRAS via ternary complex with cyclophilin A (CYPA) to block the RAS−RAF interaction62, resulted in increased CYPA binding upon STR treatment, as did KRASG13D-Y32F and KRASG13D-Y64F mutations (Fig. 5i and Extended Data Fig. 9f,g). These findings suggest that phosphorylation differentially modulates the activity of KRAS-OFF and KRAS-ON inhibitors. Consistent with our observations with P3 binding, this effect appears to be mutant specific, as STR had no effect on the ability of BI-2865 to disrupt the KRAS−RAF interaction in H1975 cells, which express KRASWT (Fig. 5i and Supplementary Fig. 8a). Finally, we observed a restoration of downstream phospho-ERK signaling when KRAS G12, G13 or Q61 mutant cells are co-treated with KRAS inhibitors and dasatinib, demonstrating that phosphorylation status influences both inhibitor binding and functional suppression (Fig. 5j and Supplementary Fig. 8b). Phosphorylation of Y32 and Y64 has been shown to impact KRAS structural conformation, impacting interactions with the effector RAF and GTP cycling56,59,63, suggesting that the observed ligandability differences may, in part, arise from phosphorylation-dependent changes around the SII region. Consistent with this, unbiased enhanced sampling molecular dynamics simulations revealed that phosphorylation at Y64 in GDP-bound KRASG13D decreases SII flexibility while increasing SI flexibility (Extended Data Fig. 10a,b and Supplementary Fig. 9). Metadynamics-derived free energy surfaces further suggested that phosphorylation at Y32 or Y64 induces a drastic shift in the conformational population, reshaping the energy landscape in a way that might favor a closed-like state compatible with BI-2865 binding (Extended Data Fig. 10c). To test whether these predicted conformational shifts directly alter BI-2865 affinity, we generated, purified and characterized non-phosphorylated, mono-phosphorylated (pY64 and pKRAS) and di-phosphorylated (pY32/pY64 and ppKRAS) KRASG12C variants (Supplementary Fig. 10a−e and Supplementary Table 15). However, surface plasmon resonance (SPR) measurements revealed no differences in BI-2865 binding affinity (Extended Data Fig. 10d−g), indicating that phosphorylation does not measurably alter intrinsic ligand affinity. We, therefore, considered whether phosphorylation modulates KRAS regulatory interactions. The crystal structure of KRAS in complex with the guanine nucleotide exchange factor SOS1 (PDB: 6EPL; Supplementary Fig. 10f) shows that Y64 participates in hydrophobic interactions at the RAS–SOS interface58,64. Previous work showed that Y64A substitution reduces RAS GTPase activity, impairs SOS interaction and diminishes SII inhibitor activity58,65,66,67, while Y32S was also reported to decrease nucleotide exchange68,69,70. We, therefore, asked whether phosphorylation at these residues modulates SOS–KRAS association. Cells expressing recombinant KRASY64F (G12C or G13D), which preserves aromaticity while preventing phosphorylation, exhibited substantially increased SOS1 binding relative to non-mutated KRAS, whereas KRASY32F showed little to no enhancement. Similarly, STR treatment significantly enhanced SOS1 association with KRAS, whereas SHP099 abrogated this effect (Fig. 5k,l and Extended Data Fig. 10h−m). Consistent with these observations, phosphorylation significantly reduced (approximately seven-fold) the rate of SOS-mediated GDP−GTP exchange (Fig. 5m,n and Extended Data Fig. 10n), with minimal effects on intrinsic exchange. Collectively, these data support a model where phosphorylation of Y64 disrupts SOS binding and SOS-mediated nucleotide exchange, enriching a GDP-bound state preferred by ligands overlapping the SII inhibitor binding site (Supplementary Fig. 10g). Although previous efforts systematically cataloged PTM prevalence and dynamics through MS5, translating these high-dimensional datasets into actionable insight regarding protein function, chemical tractability or druggability remains limited71. A limited number of examples, often discovered serendipitously, demonstrate direct PTM-dependent modulation of ligandability. For instance, unphosphorylated Abelson (Abl) tyrosine kinase exhibits over 150-fold greater sensitivity to inhibition by imatinib; acetylation of CYPA antagonizes the immunosuppressive effects of cyclosporin72,73; and α1-acid glycoprotein (AGP) exhibits glycoform-dependent binding to warfarin74. Recent chemical proteomic studies have similarly identified instances of context-dependent liganding events, highlighting the potential of this strategy to broadly assess state-dependent pharmacology8,75,76. Our approach directly addresses this gap by enabling proteome-wide assessment of how phosphorylation, N-linked glycosylation and, in principle, any PTM or cellular state can influence small-molecule−protein interactions. The implications of our findings extend broadly across biological and therapeutic contexts. PTM-dependent ligandability events span diverse protein classes, including traditionally challenging drug targets such as transcription factors and epigenetic regulators. Our data point to a wide range of mechanisms, beyond abundance changes, by which PTMs may affect protein ligandability. We observed strong enrichment of differentially liganded sites proximal to corresponding altered PTM (Fig. 4a–g), suggesting direct shaping of ligand binding pockets, either by altering pocket accessibility orthosterically or influencing conformational dynamics. We also identified distal events (Extended Data Fig. 7k−n), exemplified by LAMTOR2, where STR-mediated disruption of the Ragulator complex appears to create a ‘neo-pocket' (Fig. 4h–k). Notably, ligandability changes frequently localize to annotated functional regions (Extended Data Fig. 7a,b and Supplementary Figs. 4 and 5), including active sites, substrate-binding pockets, protein−protein interfaces as well as functionally unannotated sites, supporting the idea that ligandability can serve as a proxy for protein status—reporting shifts in conformation, interactions and other functions beyond direct small-molecule binding. Critically, our study also identified clinically relevant targets, such as KRAS, whose phosphorylation state profoundly influences inhibitor efficacy. This raises important considerations for how chemical probes and approved drugs behave across cellular states. SHP2 inhibitors impair growth and induce death in KRAS-driven cancers, and their combination with KRAS inhibitors has resulted in enhanced efficacy in clinical settings77,78. These findings have largely been attributed to coordinated suppression of upstream RTK signaling and altered KRAS GTP cycling through direct GEF and GAP modulation56,79,80,81,82,83,84,85,86,87. Extensive work has shown that phosphorylation of KRAS alters switch dynamics and perturbs GTPase cycling, leading to insensitivity to GEF-mediated and GAP-mediated regulation and impaired effector engagement56,59,63. However, our observation that phosphorylation has similar impacts to cells harboring G12/G13 mutants, as well as the Q61H mutant, suggests that additional KRAS-intrinsic factors mediated by phosphorylation may contribute to the observed effects of ligand binding to the SII region. We show that phosphorylation of KRAS, particularly at Y64, reduces interactions with SOS, leading to increased levels of GDP-bound KRAS and correspondingly enhanced engagement with SWII ligands. Wild-type KRAS exhibits opposing phosphorylation-dependent ligandability patterns relative to oncogenic mutants, and RMC-7977-mediated KRAS−CYPA stabilization is enhanced upon KRAS dephosphorylation, underscoring a multilayered regulation of pocket activity. Together, these results highlight PTM-dependent ligandability as a previously underappreciated determinant of KRAS inhibitor response and suggest opportunities to optimize therapies tailored to specific cellular states. Several limitations warrant consideration. Although we observed more than 400 PTM-dependent ligandability events, our analysis was restricted to phosphorylation and N-linked glycosylation perturbed by STR and Tuni, respectively, and thus reflects a relatively limited perspective of how PTMs and other factors might influence proteome-wide ligandability. Additionally, all experiments were conducted in HEK293T or MDA-MB-231 cells, confining our coverage to proteins expressed in these cells. Extending this approach to additional PTMs, cellular states, disease-relevant contexts and diverse tissues will further illuminate how protein status shapes ligandability and druggability. Moreover, although we employed relatively promiscuous fragment probes, our library is small and generally lipophilic and inadequately covers broad chemical space. Larger libraries with structurally diverse and stereo-chemically defined members should enable deeper examination of the proteome and facilitate discovery of state-dependent chemicals33,88,89. Integrating computational methods to model the structural impact of PTMs on small-molecule binding en masse could complement experimental efforts and perhaps enable state-dependent drug discovery de novo. Finally, although PTM databases (for example, UniProt and PhosphoSitePlus) serve as valuable resources for generating mechanistic hypotheses, these annotated sites may not always reflect the specific PTM status under considered experimental conditions, underscoring the need for targeted PTM validation on a case-by-case basis. Collectively, this work provides a framework not only to investigate how PTMs modulate protein status but also to discover chemical probes tailored to specific functional or pathological contexts. MDA-MB-231 (HTB-26), HEK293T (CRL-3216), H460 (HTB-177), MIA PaCa2 (CRL-1420) and H1975 (CRL-5908) cell lines were procured from the American Type Culture Collection; HCC44 (ACC 534) cells were obtained from DSMZ; and Lenti-X 293 T (LX293T and 632180) cells were obtained from Takara Bio. MDA-MB-231, MIA PaCa2, HEK293T and Lenti-X 293T cells were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10% (v/v) FBS and 2 mM L-glutamine. HCC44, H1975 and H460 cells were maintained in RPMI 1640 medium supplemented with 10% (v/v) FBS and 2 mM L-glutamine. All cell lines were incubated at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2. Unless otherwise indicated, all compound treatment experiments were performed when cells are around 50−70% confluency. The chemical probes and acid-cleavable biotin-azide used in this study were synthesized in-house based on previously reported research from our laboratory20,24,90. The structural identity of these resynthesized compounds was verified by 1H and 13C nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy alongside high-resolution MS. Purity of synthesized compounds was determined to be greater than 95% by analytical high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) using ultraviolet detection at 254 nm and 1H NMR. Commonly used reagents were purchased from Corning, Gibco, Bio-Rad, Research Product International, Sigma-Aldrich and Thermo Fisher Scientific. Biotin-PEG3-Azide (AZ104) was from Click Chemistry Tools. Sequencing-grade modified trypsin (V5111) was purchased from Promega. Endoproteinase LysC (P8109S) is from New England Biolabs. Pierce Streptavidin Agarose (cat. no. 20353), Pierce High pH Reversed-Phase Fractionation Kit (84868), TMT10plex Isobaric Label Reagent Set (90406), TMTpro 16plex Label Reagent Set (A44520) and Halt protease and phosphatase inhibitors (78438 or 78441) were from Thermo Fisher Scientific. Specific chemical reagents originated from the following sources without further purification: STR (Selleck Chemicals, S1421); ALW-II-41-27 (HY-18007), BI-2493 (HY-153723), BI-2865 (HY-153724), AMG-510 (HY-114277), MRTX-849 (HY-130149), RMC-7977 (HY-156498), SHP-099 (HY-100388) and dasatinib (HY-10181), all from MedChemExpress; Tuni (Merck, T7765); and cholesterol (Avanti Research, 700100). All purchased compounds have greater than 98% purity, as reported by the vendors. The full-length codon-optimized human NPC2 (including wild-type, N58A or N135A mutation) and human GABARAPL2 (wild-type and S87/88A) were synthesized (Twist Bioscience) and cloned into a pTWIST-CMV-puro vector individually. The full-length codon-optimized human TNFRSF10B−Flag was synthesized (GenScript) and cloned into a pcDNA3.1 vector individually. pBabe−HA−KRAS (75282), pRK5−p14−Flag (42330) and pRK5F−TACE (31713) were obtained from Addgene. Site-directed mutagenesis was generated by Phusion Flash High-Fidelity PCR (Thermo Fisher Scientific, F548S) and ligated by In-Fusion Snap Assembly (Takara Bio, 638947). All plasmids were verified by Sanger or whole-plasmid sequencing. For transient transfection, LX293T cells were seeded and transfected by Lipofectamine 3000 Transfection Reagent (Invitrogen, L3000075). Cells were harvested at 24 hours or 48 hours after transfection for subsequent analysis. Cells were plated at 20,000 cells per well in 96-well plates. The media were first aspirated after the cells were attached, and the cells were then treated in triplicate with media containing different concentrations of STR (24 nM−50 µM) for MDA-MB-231 cells and Tuni (1−100 µg ml−1) for HEK293T cells. The same volume of DMSO was used as a vehicle control. Cell proliferation was assayed using the CellTiter-Glo Luminescent Cell Viability Assay (Promega) following the manufacturer's guidelines. Data represent the average and standard deviation of triplicates in measured luminescence. For MDA-MB-231 cells, cells were cultured to approximately 90% confluency in 15-cm plates, pretreated with STR for 3 hours. For HEK293T cells, cells grew to about 70% confluency and were then treated with Tuni for 24 hours. After aspirating the growth medium, the cells were incubated in serum-free medium containing FFF probes (20 µM for in-gel labeling, 100 µM for proteomics, site of labeling (SoL) and gel-based validations) for 30 minutes at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere. After treatment, the cells were exposed to ultraviolet light (365 nm) for 20 minutes, harvested, washed with cold DPBS and transferred to tubes. The cell suspensions were then centrifuged (400g, 5 minutes) and washed with cold DPBS, and the resulting pellets were stored at −80 °C until further processing. Gel-based analysis of probe cross-linked proteins was performed as previously described90. In brief, 6 μl of a freshly prepared click reaction mixture was added to each 50-μl protein sample (2 mg ml−1). The click mix included 3 μl of 1.7 mM tris((1-benzyl-1H-1,2,3-triazol-4-yl)methyl)amine (TBTA) in 4:1 t-BuOH:DMSO; 1 μl of 50 mM CuSO4 in water; 1 μl of 1.25 mM tetramethylrhodamine (TAMRA) azide in DMSO; and 1 μl of freshly prepared 50 mM tris(2-carboxyethyl)phosphine (TCEP) in DPBS. Samples were mixed with the click mix and incubated at room temperature for 1 hour. Reactions were then quenched with 17 μl of 4× SDS sample buffer. Proteins (15 μg per lane) were resolved by SDS-PAGE using in-house prepared 10% acrylamide gels and visualized via in-gel fluorescence with a ChemiDoc Imaging System (Bio-Rad). Images were processed using Image Lab software (Bio-Rad). For unenriched proteomic samples, cell pellets were resuspended in 100 μl of ice-cold DPBS containing 1× protease inhibitor cocktail (Thermo Fisher Scientific, 78441) and lysed by probe sonication (Branson Sonifier; 15 ms on, 40 ms off, 15% amplitude, seven pulses). Protein concentrations were measured using the BCA assay (Thermo Fisher Scientific). Then, 100−200 μg of protein was denatured in 8 M urea, reduced with 5 mM dithiothreitol (DTT) and alkylated with 55 mM iodoacetamide (IAA). Proteins were precipitated with 4:1 methanol:chloroform, washed and resuspended in 100 mM TEAB. Sequential digestion was performed using LysC (New England Biolabs, P8109S) for 2 hours, followed by trypsin (Promega, V5111) overnight at 37 °C in the presence of 1 mM CaCl2. Peptides were quantified using the Pierce Quantitative Fluorometric Peptide Assay (Thermo Fisher Scientific) and labeled with TMT10plex reagents (Thermo Fisher Scientific) in 30% acetonitrile. Labeling was quenched with 6 μl of 5% hydroxylamine (v/v), followed by a 15-minute incubation. Samples were acidified with 8 μl of formic acid, dried and resuspended in 0.1% trifluoroacetic acid (TFA). Fractionation was carried out using the Pierce High pH Reversed-Phase Fractionation Kit (Thermo Fisher Scientific, 84868). MDA-MB-231 peptides were divided into 18 fractions and pooled into nine sets; HEK293T peptides were divided into 28 fractions and pooled into 14 sets. All fractions were dried and stored at −80 °C until liquid chromatography−mass spectrometry (LC−MS) analysis. For phosphoproteomic samples, MDA-MB-231 cells were treated with 0.25 μM STR for 3 hours prior to harvest. Cell pellets were lysed in 1 ml of ice-cold DPBS containing protease and phosphatase inhibitors (Thermo Fisher Scientific, 78441) and sonicated. Proteins were denatured with 4 M urea, reduced (DTT, 5 mM), alkylated (IAA, 55 mM) and precipitated. Pellets were resuspended in 1 M urea in 50 mM HEPES (pH 8.5), digested with LysC and then trypsin and desalted using Sep-Pak C18 cartridges. Approximately 2 mg of peptides per sample was enriched using TiO2 beads (GL Sciences, 5010-21315) or Fe-NTA beads (Thermo Fisher Scientific, A32992), following manufacturer protocols. For TiO2 enrichment, peptides were resuspended in 2 M lactic acid and 50% acetonitrile and incubated with beads at a 1:4 peptide:bead ratio for 1 hour at room temperature with vortexing. Beads were washed three times with binding buffer and three times with wash buffer (50% acetonitrile, 0.1% TFA). Phosphopeptides were eluted with 50 mM potassium phosphate (pH 10) and desalted using Sep-Pak C18 cartridges. The eluates from the TiO2 or Fe-NTA enrichment were dried in a vacuum centrifuge and then labeled with TMT10plex reagents (Thermo Fisher Scientific) for 1 hour at room temperature, quenched, acidified and dried. Then, peptides were resuspended in 0.1% TFA, pooled and fractionated using the Pierce High pH Reversed-Phase Fractionation Kit into 18 fractions (7.5−95% acetonitrile), which were pooled into nine final sets, dried and stored at −80 °C. KRAS phosphoproteomics sample preparation was performed using the S-Trap micro spin column protocol. In brief, 5 µg of purified KRAS protein was solubilized in 130 µl of buffer containing 50 mM TEAB (pH 7.5) and 5% SDS. Proteins were reduced with 5 mM DTT at 56 °C for 30 minutes and subsequently alkylated with 50 mM IAA for 30 minutes in dark. The sample was acidified with 15 µl of 12% phosphoric acid, followed by the addition of 900 µl of S-Trap binding buffer (90% methanol, 100 mM TEAB (pH 7.1)). The mixture was loaded onto an S-Trap micro spin column and centrifuged at 10,000g for 30 seconds. The column was washed three times with 225 µl of S-Trap binding buffer. For digestion, 5 µg of trypsin reconstituted in 100 mM TEAB (pH 8.5) was added to the column and incubated overnight at 37 °C. Peptides were eluted sequentially with 40 µl of 50 mM TEAB (pH 8.0), 40 µl of 0.2% formic acid and 40 µl of 50% acetonitrile/0.2% formic acid. Elutions were collected by centrifugation at 4,000g for 1 minute. The combined eluates were dried using a vacuum concentrator prior to analysis. Chemoproteomic samples were prepared in biological duplicates for each condition using previously described procedures91. In brief, cell pellets were resuspended in 500 µl of cold DPBS and lysed via sonication. Protein concentrations were normalized to 2 mg ml−1 in 500 µl of ice-cold DPBS, using a DC Protein Assay (Bio-Rad), in 15-ml Falcon tubes. The ‘click chemistry cocktail' was then added to each lysate, consisting of TBTA (final concentration: 100 µM), TCEP (final concentration: 1 mM), biotin-azide (final concentration: 100 µM) and CuSO4 (final concentration: 1 mM). Samples were incubated at room temperature for 1 hour. Ice-cold 4:1 MeOH:CHCl3 (2.5 ml) and ice-cold DPBS (1 ml) were added to each sample to quench the click reaction, followed by centrifugation (3,200g, 4 °C, 10 minutes) to precipitate proteins. The precipitates were washed with 4:1 MeOH:CHCl3 and resuspended in 500 µl of 6 M urea in DPBS, along with 10 µl of 10% SDS. Protein reduction was performed by adding 50 µl of a freshly prepared 1:1 solution of TCEP (25 µl, 200 mM in DPBS) and K2CO3 (25 µl, 600 mM in DPBS) and incubated at 37 °C for 30 minutes. After reduction, proteins were alkylated by adding 70 µl of freshly prepared IAA (400 mM in DPBS) and incubated for 30 minutes at room temperature in dark. After alkylation, 130 µl of 10% SDS was added to the samples, followed by dilution with 5.5 ml of DPBS. A 100-µl aliquot of 50% streptavidin agarose slurry (Thermo Fisher Scientific, 20349) was added to each tube, and samples were rotated for approximately 1.5 hours at room temperature to enrich probe-labeled proteins. The beads were then centrifuged down to tube bottom (750g, 2 minutes, 4 °C) and washed once with 0.2% SDS in DPBS (5 minutes), twice with DPBS and once with water and transferred to LoBind microcentrifuge tubes with 100 mM TEAB (pH 8.5). Bound proteins were digested overnight with trypsin (Promega, V5111) in digestion buffer (100 ml, 100 mM TEAB (pH 8.5) and 100 µM CaCl2) at 37 °C. The resulting peptides were transferred to new tubes for each condition and labeled with the respective TMT (Thermo Fisher Scientific) 10plex or 16plex reagents (8 µl, 20 µg µl−1) for 1 hour, quenched, acidified and dried through a SpeedVac vacuum concentrator. The dried peptides were redissolved in 0.1% TFA in water, combined and fractionated into 12 fractions by Pierce High pH Reversed-Phase Fractionation Kit. The fractions were pooled pairwise into six final fractions, dried and stored at −80 °C until MS analysis. The preparation of SoL samples followed our previously reported protocol21. In brief, cell pellets were resuspended in cold DPBS (1,000 µl) and lysed via sonication. Then, 3 mg of the lysates was taken and diluted into 1 ml of DPBS in 15-ml Falcon tubes. Each sample was then added with TBTA (final concentration: 100 µM), TCEP (final concentration: 1 mM), cleavable biotin-azide tag (light L-valine tag, final concentration: 100 µM) and CuSO4 (final concentration: 1 mM) for 1 hour. Cold methanol (4 ml) was added, and the protein was allowed to precipitate overnight. The precipitated proteins were pelleted by centrifugation (4,000g, 10 minutes), and the supernatant was discarded. The pellets were washed with 4:1 (v/v) MeOH:CHCl3, resuspended by sonication and centrifuged again (4,000g, 10 minutes). The pellets were then resuspended in freshly prepared 6 M urea solution (1,000 µl, in DPBS) and a solution of SDS (100 µl, 10% w/v), followed by reduction and alkylation steps as described above. The samples were diluted with DPBS (11 ml); streptavidin-agarose slurry (200 µl, 50%, Pierce) was added; and the tubes were rotated for 1.5 hours at room temperature. The beads were pelleted by centrifugation (500g, 5 minutes) and washed with 0.2% SDS in DPBS (1×), DPBS (2×), water (1×) and 100 mM TEAB (2 × 5 ml (pH 8.5) in water). The beads were then transferred to 1.5-ml LoBind microcentrifuge tubes, added with sequencing-grade modified trypsin (4 µg, 100 mM TEAB (pH 8.5), 100 µM CaCl2) and incubated overnight at 37 °C. The beads were further washed with SDS in DPBS once (0.2% w/v), NaCl in DPBS (2×, 150 mM) and water (2×) by centrifugation (400g, 5 minutes). Elution was performed with a buffer of formic acid/water (3% v/v, 200 µl) for 1 hour with gentle shaking. The supernatant was collected, and the beads were re-eluted with the same buffer, followed by an additional elution with acetonitrile/water (50% v/v, with 3% formic acid, 200 µl). The supernatants were combined and dried using vacuum centrifugation. The resulting residue was reconstituted in TEAB (100 mM) and labeled with TMT reagents (Thermo Fisher Scientific) across replicates as per the manufacturer's instructions. TMT-labeled samples were pooled, dried, fractionated into 12 fractions by Pierce High pH Reversed-Phase Fractionation Kit, combined into six final fractions, dried and stored at −80 °C until MS analysis. LC−MS analysis was conducted following our previously published protocol21. For phosphor, unenriched and enriched proteomics, samples were resuspended in MS sample buffer (20 µl, 5% acetonitrile, 0.1% formic acid in water) prior to LC−MS analysis, following published methods91. In short, 3 µl of each sample was loaded onto an Acclaim PepMap 100 precolumn (75 µm × 2 mm) and separated on a PepMap RSLC analytical column (2 µm, 100 Å, 75 µm × 25 cm) using an UltiMate 3000 RSLCnano system (Thermo Fisher Scientific). Buffer A (0.1% formic acid in water) and buffer B (0.1% formic acid in acetonitrile) were used in a 220-minute gradient at a flow rate of 300 nl min−1. The gradient consisted of 2% buffer B for the first 10 minutes, a gradual increase to 30% buffer B over the next 192 minutes, 60% buffer B for 5 minutes, followed by a ramp to 95% buffer B in 1 minute, hold for 5 minutes, then back to 2% buffer B in 1 minute and re-equilibrated for 6 minutes. Eluents were analyzed using an Orbitrap Fusion Lumos mass spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific) with a 3-second cycle time and a nano-LC electrospray ionization voltage of 2,000 V. Data were acquired with Xcalibur software (version 4.1.50). MS1 spectra were captured with a scan range of 375−1,500 m/z, a maximum injection time of 50 ms and dynamic exclusion enabled (one repeat, exclusion duration of 20 seconds). The resolution was set to 120,000 with an automatic gain control (AGC) target of 1 × 106. Peptides chosen for MS2 analysis were fragmented by collision-induced dissociation (CID) at 30% collision energy at 1.6-m/z isolation window. Fragment ions were recorded in the ion trap (AGC target 1.8 × 104, maximum injection time of 120 ms). MS3 spectra were generated using high-energy collision dissociation (HCD) mode with 65% collision energy, and synchronous precursor selection (SPS) was set as 10. For SoL proteomics, samples were resuspended in 30 μl of MS sample buffer, and 10 μl was injected onto the analytical column. The loading phase involved 2% buffer B (0.1% formic acid in acetonitrile) for 10 minutes, followed by elution with a gradient of 2−35% buffer B over 215 minutes, 35−55% over 10 minutes, 55–95% over 5 minutes, 95% buffer B for 2 minutes and then decreasing to 2% buffer B over 1 minute. After holding at 2% for 2 minutes, the gradient was ramped to 95% buffer B in 1 minute, held at 95% for 2 minutes, decreased again to 2% in 1 minute and held at 2% for an additional 11 minutes, totaling 260 minutes of run time. Eluents were analyzed using an Orbitrap Fusion Lumos mass spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific). MS1 scans were performed with a resolution of 120,000, a scan range of 375−2,000 m/z and an RF lens setting of 60%, with a dynamic exclusion window of 30 seconds. MS2 scans used the Orbitrap mass analyzer with a resolution of 15,000 and a first mass of 120. Peptide isolation and fragmentation were carried out with quadrupole isolation of 1.6 m/z, an AGC target of 5 × 104, a maximum injection time of 110 ms and an HCD energy of 28%. For MS3, the Orbitrap mass analyzer was set to a resolution of 50,000 with an AGC target of 1.5 × 105, a maximum injection time of 120 ms and an HCD collision energy of 65% (SPS 10). Proteomic analysis was carried out using Proteome Discoverer (version 3.0, Thermo Fisher Scientific) following previously established protocols91. For phosphor, unenriched and enriched proteomic results, peptide identification was conducted using the SEQUEST HT algorithm; precursor mass tolerance is 10 ppm; and fragment mass tolerances is 0.6 Da, allowing for two missed cleavage sites. Variable modifications included oxidation (methionine, +15.994915) and carbamidomethyl (cysteine, +57.02146), and TMT modifications (lysine and N-terminal, +229.1629 for 10plex, +304.207 for 16plex tags) were used as fixed modifications. The spectral data were searched against the Homo sapiens proteome database (UniProt 2018; 42,358 sequences), applying a 1% false discovery rate (FDR) with Percolator. MS3 peptide quantification was performed using a mass tolerance of 20 ppm. For unenriched proteomic analysis, abundances in each channel were normalized to the average of the median signal intensity across all channels. For phosphoproteomic analysis, no normalization was applied to peptide abundances. For enriched proteomic results, abundances in each channel were normalized to endogenously biotinylated proteins (PCCA, PC, MCCC1 or ACACA). For phospho-dependent experiments P2 and P6, no normalization was applied to protein abundances. SoL analysis was conducted according to the previously published protocol21, using Proteome Discoverer (version 3.0, Thermo Fisher Scientific) for data processing. Peptide sequences were identified by comparing experimental fragmentation patterns with proteome databases via the SEQUEST HT algorithm. The precursor mass tolerance was set to 10 ppm; fragment mass tolerance was set to 0.05 Da; and up to two missed cleavage sites were allowed. Static modifications included TMT at the N terminus (+229.163), and variable modifications specified were carbamidomethyl (cysteine, +57.02146), oxidation (methionine, +15.994915), TMT tag (lysine and N-terminal, +229.1629 for 10plex, +304.207 for 16plex tags) and FFF tags (P2: +481.310; P3: +501.274; P4: +467.217; P5: +517.305; P6: +453.274) on all amino acids. The spectral data were searched against each probe's enrichment database from the whole protein profiling experiment with a 0.01 FDR using the target−decoy approach. Further validation was performed using Percolator, applying the target−decoy strategy with a ‘Separate' setting and a maximum ΔCn threshold of 0.1. TMT quantification was carried out at the MS3 level, with a tolerance of 20 ppm. Only peptides with an average signal-to-noise ratio greater than 10 were retained for further analysis. For MSFragger SoL searches, an open search workflow was used92. In brief, precursor mass tolerance was set to 0–520 Da, and MS1 tolerance was set to 20 ppm. Full trypsin specificity was designated with a maximum of two missed cleavages. Peptide length was set from 4 to 50; peptide mass range was set from 500 to 5,000. Oxidation (methionine, +15.9949), carbamidomethyl (cysteine, +57.02146) and TMT tag (lysine, +229.1629 for 10plex, +304.207 for 16plex tags) were used as variable modifications, and TMT tags (N-terminal, +229.1629 for 10plex, +304.207 for 16plex tags) were used as fixed modifications. To ensure data reliability, proteins reported required at least two unique peptides. TMT ratios calculated in Proteome Discoverer were transformed using log2(x), and statistical significance of the ratios was assessed with two-tailed Student's t-tests across two biological replicates (significance threshold set at P < 0.05). Detailed proteomics data are available in Supplementary Tables 1–15. Cells were treated as described in the ‘Photoaffinity probe cell treatments' section. Probe-labeled cell pellets were lysed in ice-cold DPBS supplemented with 1× Halt protease inhibitor cocktail (Thermo Fisher Scientific, 78438) and lysed by sonication. Protein concentrations were normalized and click reactions were performed as described in the chemoproteomic part in the ‘Preparation of samples for proteomic analysis' section. In brief, after the ‘click chemistry' reaction, 2 ml of ice-cold MeOH was added to each sample to precipitate proteins and incubated overnight at −20 °C. The samples were then centrifuged (3,200g, 4 °C, 10 minutes) and washed with 4:1 MeOH:CHCl3, resuspended by sonication and centrifuged as above. Pellets were resuspended in freshly prepared urea solution (500 µl of DPBS, 6 M) with SDS (100 µl of 10% w/v). After incubating the samples for 30 minutes at 37 °C to fully dissolve the protein pellet, samples were diluted with DPBS (5.5 ml), added with a streptavidin-agarose slurry (100 µl, 50%, Pierce) and rotated for 1.5 hours at room temperature. The beads were pelleted by centrifugation (500g, 5 minutes, 4 °C) and washed once with DPBS with 0.2% SDS, washed three times with DPBS and then boiled with 2× SDS sample buffer for 15 minutes. Boiled samples and saved input samples were resolved by SDS-PAGE and followed by immunoblotting analysis. Protein enrichment analysis was conducted and modified following an established protein enrichment clustering workflow23. In brief, enriched proteins were analyzed and clustered based on protein classifications (PANTHER database 19.0 and UniProt ‘Family and domain databases')93. Significant and emerging significant hits were identified based on ligandability changes. Proteins were visualized in clusters reflecting functional and classification similarities. Downstream analyses included Gene Ontology term and Illuminating the Druggable Genome (IDG) category enrichment, using PANTHER family annotations and IDG database annotations, respectively. Clustering and plotting were performed using Python scripts adapted from previously published work23. Protein information for the labeled peptides was used to extract additional information from UniProt, such as structural features (ACT_SITE, BINDING, METAL, DNA_BIND and NP_BIND), presence in DrugBank, Gene Ontology keywords and PDB and AlphaFold structure identities. Protein structures were batch downloaded from PDB or AlphaFold using command line prompts in terminal. Proteomic, pocket, site and structure data were processed together using R scripts adapted from previously published work21. For NPC2 localization, HEK293T cells were seeded on 0.02% poly-L-lysine (Sigma-Aldrich, P1274)-coated eight-well plates (ibidi, 80827) at 37 °C overnight. Cells were treated with Tuni for another 24 hours, fixed in Bouin's solution (Sigma-Aldrich) for 10 minutes and permeabilized with ice-cold methanol at −20 °C for 10 minutes. After fixation/permeabilization, cells were washed for 5 minutes with PBS and then blocked with 3% BSA/PBST (PBS with 0.05% Triton X-100) for 10 minutes. Primary antibodies were diluted with blocking buffer (rabbit anti-NPC2 1:100 and mouse anti-LAMP1 1:500) and incubated with cells for 1 hour. After three washes with PBST, cells were incubated with Alexa Fluor-labeled secondary antibodies (1:500) for an additional hour. Cells were then washed three times with PBST and stained with NucBlue probe (Thermo Fisher Scientific, R37606) for nuclear imaging. Confocal images were carried out on a Zeiss LSM 780 microscope using a ×100 objective (numerical aperture = 1.40), and 1 Airy unit was set as a pinhole for each channel. Imaris, ImageJ and ZEN (Zeiss) software were used for image processing and analysis. MDA-MB-231 cells were digested with 0.25% trypsin, and equal amounts of cells were incubated with DMSO, BI-2865 (10 nM) or 6-aminocoumarin (100 µM) with or without STR/SHP-099/dasatinib in fresh DMEM media for 30 minutes at 37 °C. Cells were then collected and washed once with binding buffer (cold DPBS containing DMSO or the indicated concentration of inhibitors and 1× Halt protease inhibitor cocktail). Cells were then resuspended in binding buffer, counted and aliquoted to a concentration of 300,000 cells per 50 μl before being transferred to polymerase chain reaction (PCR) tubes for heat treatment. PCR tubes were heated at the indicated temperature gradient on a thermal cycler (Bio-Rad) for 3 minutes, followed by incubation at 25 °C for 3 minutes. After three snap freeze–thaw cycles with liquid nitrogen, lysates were transferred to 1.5-ml Eppendorf tubes and centrifuged at 100,000gfor 20 minutes at 4 °C to separate destabilized proteins from proteins remaining in solution. The resulting supernatant was transferred to new Eppendorf tubes, mixed with 4× SDS sample buffer, boiled for 5 minutes and processed for immunoblotting as described below. RAS-binding domain (RBD) experiments were performed using the Active Ras Pull-Down and Detection Kit (Thermo Fisher Scientific, 16117) following the manufacturer's instructions. In brief, after compound treatment for the specified duration, cells were washed with ice-cold PBS, harvested, snap frozen in liquid nitrogen and stored at −80 °C until use. Cells were lysed in lysis buffer, vortexed and incubated on ice for 15 minutes. The lysates were then centrifuged at 14,000g for 15 minutes at 4 °C, and supernatants were collected. Cell lysates were incubated with Raf-GST and GST beads at 4 °C for 1.5 hours with rotation, washed three times with lysis buffer and eluted by incubating in 2× sample buffer for 15 minutes. All samples were resolved by SDS-PAGE and processed for immunoblotting as described below. For immunoblotting, cells were treated with indicated compounds for specified time and then washed with ice-cold PBS and lysed in NP-40 buffer (10 mM Tris-HCl, 150 mM NaCl, 1 mM EDTA and 1% (w/v) NP-40) supplemented with protease and phosphatase inhibitors. After centrifuging at 14,000g for 10 minutes at 4 °C to remove cell debris, supernatants were taken, and protein concentrations were determined by a DC Protein Assay (Bio-Rad, 5000112). Equal amounts of protein lysates were mixed with the 4× SDS sample buffer and resolved by SDS-PAGE. For LAMTOR2−Flag immunoprecipitation, cells were resuspended in E1C buffer (50 mM HEPES, 250 mM NaCl, 5 mM EDTA and 0.3% (w/v) CHAPS supplemented with protease inhibitor) and sonicated three times. Lysates were cleared by centrifugation and normalized to 1.5−2.0 mg ml−1. Then, 1 µg of anti-Flag antibody was added to supernatants and rotated overnight at 4 °C and incubated with 20 μl of protein A/G agarose beads (MedChemExpress) for an additional hour on the next day. The beads were washed three times with the E1C buffer and eluted by incubating in 2× sample buffer at 95 °C for 10 minutes and resolved by SDS-PAGE. For TACE−Flag immunoprecipitation, cells were resuspended in 1% NP-40 buffer supplemented with protease inhibitor. Lysates were cleared by centrifugation and normalized to 1.5−2.0 mg ml−1. Then, 1 µg of anti-Flag antibody was added to supernatants and rotated overnight at 4 °C and incubated with 20 μl of protein A/G agarose beads (MedChemExpress) for an additional hour on the next day. The beads were washed three times with the E1C buffer and eluted by incubating in 2× sample buffer at 95 °C for 10 minutes and resolved by SDS-PAGE. For HA−KRAS immunoprecipitation, after compound treatment for the designated time, cells were washed twice with ice-cold PBS and lysed in NP-40 buffer supplemented with protease and phosphatase inhibitors. Lysates were then cleared by centrifugation, normalized and incubated with 20 µl of anti-HA magnetic beads at 4 °C for 3 hours or overnight with rotation. The beads were washed three times with lysis buffer and eluted with 2× sample buffer. Resolved proteins were transferred to PVDF membranes using the Trans-Blot Turbo Transfer system (Bio-Rad). Membranes were blocked with 5% non-fat milk or 3% BSA in TBST and incubated overnight with primary antibodies. Blots were washed three times with TBST and incubated with secondary antibodies for 1 hour. Membrane visualization was performed using the ChemiDoc Imaging System (Bio-Rad), and band density was quantified with Image Lab software (version 6.1.0). The His6-MBP-TEV-Avi-Hs.KRASG12C plasmid was transformed into Escherichia coli BL21(DE3) cells. Cultures were grown at 37 °C to an optical density at 600 nm (OD600) ≈ 0.6, and then 0.5 mM IPTG was added. The culture was continued for 20 hours at 18 °C. Cells were harvested by centrifugation, resuspended in lysis buffer (100 mM HEPES (pH 7.4), 500 mM NaCl and 25 mM imidazole) and lysed via sonication. The recombinant protein was purified using Ni-NTA affinity chromatography. After purification, the fusion protein was dialyzed using a PD-10 desalting column (GE Healthcare, 17085101). The His6-MBP tag was cleaved by incubation with HQ-TEV protease (ProTEV Plus; Promega) for 1 hour at room temperature. Both the cleaved His6-MBP tag and the protease were removed by passing the mixture through Ni-NTA spin columns (NEBExpress, S1427L). KRAS was biotinylated using a commercial biotinylation kit (Avidity LLC). In brief, 3.2 mg (approximately 137 nmol) of MBP-cleaved KRAS in 4 ml of assay buffer (100 mM HEPES and 150 mM NaCl (pH 7.4)) was treated with 500 µl of Biomix A, 500 µl of Biomix B and 13.8 µg of BirA. The final reaction conditions included 10 mM ATP, 10 mM MgOAc2, 50 µM biotin and 27 µM KRAS. The mixture was incubated for 3 hours at room temperature and subsequently buffer exchanged twice with assay buffer via a PD-10 desalting column. To generate fluorescently labeled KRAS for binding assays, the purified protein was loaded with BODIPY-GDP (Invitrogen, G22360) as described previously94. KRAS protein was diluted to 2 µM in EDTA buffer (20 mM HEPES (pH 7.5), 50 mM NaCl, 10 mM EDTA and 0.01% Tween 20) and incubated at room temperature for 1 hour to strip native nucleotides. This mixture was then diluted to 90 nM in assay buffer (20 mM HEPES (pH 7.5), 150 mM NaCl, 10 mM MgCl2 and 0.005% Tween 20) containing 900 nM BODIPY-GDP (Invitrogen, G22360) and incubated at 4 °C for overnight. Excess BODIPY-GDP was removed using a PD-10 column, and the protein was concentrated via ultracentrifugation. In vitro phosphorylation of purified recombinant human KRASG12C was carried out following a previously reported procedure56. KRAS (450 µg) was incubated with 6.8 µg of active human Src (Sigma-Aldrich, 14-326) and ATP (3 mM) in 600 µl of kinase buffer (50 mM HEPES (pH 7.5), 10 mM MgCl2, 1 mM EGTA and 0.01% Brij-35) for overnight at room temperature. The un-phosphorylated, mono-phosphorylated and di-phosphorylated forms of KRAS were separated by anion exchange chromatography using a WAX-10 column (Thermo Fisher Scientific) with mobile phases consisting of buffer A (20 mM HEPES (pH 7.0), 5 mM MgCl2 and 1 mM TCEP) and buffer B (20 mM HEPES (pH 7.0), 5 mM MgCl2, 1 mM TCEP and 1 M NaCl), both supplemented with phosphatase inhibitors (1 mM activated sodium vanadate, 2 mM imidazole and 1 mM sodium fluoride) to stabilize the phosphorylated forms56. Separation was performed at a flow rate of 1 ml min−1 with a gradient of 0–5% B (0–20 minutes), 5–18% B (20–70 minutes), 18–80% B (70–72 minutes) and 80–100% B (72–80 minutes). Isolated KRAS fractions were concentrated via ultracentrifugation, desalted using a Zeba micro spin desalting column (Thermo Fisher Scientific, 89877) and characterized by Phos-tag gel electrophoresis and phosphoproteomics. Binding of BI-2865 to different phosphorylation forms of KRAS was evaluated by SPR (Cytiva, Biacore S200). In brief, biotinylated KRAS constructs were diluted to 0.3 µM in immobilization buffer (1× HBS-P+ containing 100 nM BODIPY-GDP and 1 mM MgCl2) and injected over a streptavidin sensor chip surface until the target immobilization levels (4,000−4,500 resonance units) were reached. Flow cells were subsequently quenched with three 60-second injections of 50 µM biotin-PEG at 10 µl min−1. Binding experiments were conducted in running buffer consisting of 1× HBS-P+ supplemented with 100 nM GDP, 1 mM MgCl2, 2% DMSO and an inhibitor cocktail (0.5 mM activated sodium vanadate, 1 mM imidazole, 1 mM sodium fluoride and 1 mM sodium molybdate). BI-2865 doses were injected over all three protein channels, as well as a reference surface, simultaneously. Resulting sensorgrams were reference subtracted, blank subtracted and subsequently analyzed using Biacore Evaluation Software to determine kinetic parameters from 1:1 binding model fits. BODIPY-GDP preloaded KRAS proteins were diluted to 1 µM in reaction buffer (50 mM HEPES (pH 7.4), 150 mM NaCl and 2.5 mM MgCl2) containing 10 µM GTP and SOS1 (BPS Bioscience, 101573). All assays were performed in 384-well plates. Condition screening was conducted in duplicate using un-phosphorylated KRAS with SOS1 concentrations ranging from 2 nM to 500 nM, whereas quantitative kinetic analysis was performed in triplicate on un-phosphorylated, mono-phosphorylated and di-phosphorylated forms using 100 nM SOS1. Positive controls (containing 1 mM EDTA in the absence of SOS1) and negative controls (without both SOS1 and GTP) were included for each KRAS variant. Fluorescence was monitored using a fluorescence microplate reader with excitation at 470 nm and emission at 525 nm over time (0−220 minutes for condition screening; 0−60 minutes for quantitative kinetic analysis). Final data were normalized by setting the negative control value at 0 minutes as 100% and the positive control value at the final timepoint (220 minutes for screening; 60 minutes for kinetic analysis) as 0%. Three-dimensional coordinates of the probes were generated from SMILES using the RDKit's ETKDG version 3 followed by universal force field (UFF) energy minimization. Target protein structures were obtained from the PDB when available; otherwise, AlphaFold2-predicted models were used. In cases where multiple PDB structures existed, the most suitable was selected based on the presence of the SoL residue coordinates, highest sequence coverage and best resolution, with a preference for X-ray crystallography over cryogenic electron microscopy. In some specific cases, the structures were manually selected. These cases include KRASG13D and KRASG12C (PDB: 8B00 and 8AZV, respectively) and EphA2 (PDB: 5EK7). Hydrogen atoms were added using Reduce, and both ligands and receptors were prepared according to the standard AutoDock protocol95. Druggable pockets were identified using fpocket, and a docking grid box was defined for each, encompassing the pocket region with a minimum side length of 24 Å. All probes were docked into each predicted pocket using AutoDock-GPU96, employing default parameters for the Lamarckian Genetic Algorithm and ADADELTA local search to generate 50 poses per ligand. Pockets were ranked based on the best binding energy, and the proximity (<6 Å) of the diazirine moiety to any proteomics-identified adducted residue was used as a criterion for successful labeling. Pocket prediction success was defined as the percentage of docking events meeting these criteria across the entire probe library, and site prediction success considered only probes experimentally observed in proteomics. Molecular visualization of docking results was performed using ChimeraX 1.8. Structural models of GDP-bound KRAS were generated via homology modeling (SwissModel) to ensure consistency with K-Ras4A (UniProt ID: P01116). Crystallographic water molecules were retained, and protonation states were assigned at pH 7.4. Molecular dynamics simulations were performed in OpenMM 8.1.2 using Amber ff14SB for the protein and the recently published phosaa14SB for phosphotyrosine. Ligands were parameterized via Espaloma 0.3.2, and modified 12-6-4 Lennard-Jones parameters were used for divalent metal ions. Systems were solvated with TIP3P-FB water molecules and 0.15 M NaCl. A Langevin Middle Integrator was used with a 4-fs timestep combining HMR and SHAKE algorithms. Harmonic positional restraints were applied on all heavy atoms, and the systems were gradually heated to 300 K in the NVT ensemble. Subsequently, a Monte Carlo barostat was introduced, and 15 steps of NPT equilibration were performed, progressively releasing the restraints. Finally, 50 ns of unrestrained molecular dynamics simulations were conducted to ensure proper equilibration. The experimental structure of KRASG13D bound to the BI-2865 compound (PDB: 8B00) was simulated for 100 ns, and the average of the last 50 ns was used as a reference. The root mean square deviation (RMSD) to the BI-2865 binding competent conformation was calculated over the Cα atoms of residues within 4 Å of the ligand. An ad hoc computational sampling pipeline was implemented to explore the conformational landscape of the systems efficiently. Initially, well-tempered metadynamics simulations (WTMetaD) were conducted, biasing the RMSD of Cα atoms relative to the initial conformation. Gaussian potentials of height 0.3 kcal mol−1 and width 0.01 nm were deposited every 2 ps with a bias factor of 10. Three simulations of 300 ns were run in parallel, sharing the bias history (that is, multiple-walker scheme). Then, all Cα pairwise distances (13,041 features) were calculated from the trajectories, and MoSAIC analysis was used to identify clusters of highly correlated motions. Features from the top three bigger clusters were subjected to principal component analysis, retaining the components that explained 95% of the variance. Regular space clustering in the reduced space was conducted to achieve uniform sampling, and 30 representative conformations were extracted for each system. Each state was equilibrated, followed by 200 ns of unbiased molecular dynamics. WTMetaD simulations were performed with PLUMED 2.9 using G12−T35 and G35−G60 minimum distances as collective variables. Gaussians (0.5 kcal mol−1 height, 0.01 nm width) were deposited every 1 ps with a bias factor of 12. Four independent replicas of 100 ns were simulated per system and combined using the mean force integration (MFI) method to obtain the free energy surfaces. All fluorescent gel scanning and western blots were performed with a minimum of two biological replicates. Compound effects at different doses were normalized to vehicle-treated cells and fitted with Sigmoidal 4PL curve. All statistical analysis was performed using GraphPad Prism (version 9.3.0 or version 10.4.0) or Microsoft Excel (version 2122). Proteomic data were statistically evaluated using two-tailed Student's t-tests performed across a minimum of two biological replicates, with significance defined as P < 0.05. Further information on research design is available in the Nature Portfolio Reporting Summary linked to this article. The data that support the findings in this work are available within the paper and the Supplementary Information (Supplementary Text and Supplementary Tables 1–15). Uncropped, full western blot images and fluorescent gels are provided in Source data and Supplementary Figs. 9–12. All raw proteomics data files have been deposited to the PRIDE repository and are available under accession numbers PXD056973 (unenriched and phosphorproteomics), PXD056976 (chemoproteomics) and PXD056980 (SoL). The simulation input PDB models necessary to reproduce the results in this study have been deposited in the Zenodo97 public repository and are accessible under the digital object identifier https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19054682. Source data are provided with this paper. The scripts used to run and analyze the simulations in this study are part of an in-house software package currently under active development by the Forli laboratory. Because the complete software suite remains unpublished and is undergoing final development, the specific code necessary to reproduce the reported results is available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request. Upon the official release of the software, the full package will be made publicly accessible through the Forli laboratory GitHub repository at https://github.com/forlilab. Huang, X. et al. Ordoukhanian from the Biophysics and Biochemistry Core at Scripps Research for SPR. We also thank B. F. Cravatt for useful discussions and input. The research of C.G.P. in this area is supported by National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases grants R01AI156268 and R01AI182439 as well as grant 2023-332369 from the Chan Zuckerberg Initiative DAF, an advised fund of the Silicon Valley Community Foundation.
